notes.anchor: I would discharge for you the office you discharged for me in my own bereavement
notes.note: Cicero refers to Brutus's consolation of him after the death of his daughter Tullia in February 45 BC, when Brutus wrote him a letter of philosophical comfort; Cicero now attempts to return the favor.

notes.anchor: to the people and to the stage, as the saying goes
notes.note: "Populo et scaenae serviendum" echoes a proverbial sentiment: the man in public life must perform for his audience (the Roman people) as an actor for a stage; Brutus's grief, unlike a private man's, is a public spectacle that affects morale.

notes.anchor: what you have lost had no equal on earth
notes.note: The bereavement Cicero consoles is almost certainly the death of Porcia, Brutus's wife (daughter of Cato), who died in 43 BC; Cicero tactfully does not name her.

notes.anchor: in the letter I was meaning to entrust to our old friend
notes.note: A separate, longer political letter Cicero intends to send via a trusted mutual courier (the "old friend" unnamed here); such bearer references were common when sensitive political content was involved.
